In the third world, textile giants do not always control their suppliers. And in recent years, scandals multiplied. In 2005, the collapse of the spectrum factory as our subtractor killed 64 in Bangladesh the workers worked seven days a week for 10 euros per month in detect set up a compensation fund for victims four years later in 2011 Zara is still getting talked about, but this time in South America. In Brazil, some of its suppliers employ workers in slave-like conditions. And in Argentina, the Alameda Foundation, which fights against those types of abuses, filmed raids in sordid sweatshops. Inditex acknowledged these irregularities, specifying that suppliers use those workers and methods without informing them. To clean up its image, Inditex strengthened the Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers in 2012. It states that no forced labor, no child labor labor no harsh or inhumane treatment the right to remuneration reasonable working hours environmental pledge to see how it works we went to North Africa in Tunisia where wages are lower than in China and where inditex has many subcontractors the main textile production center is near monastir 200 kilometers away from tunis 450 government enterprises are gathered there we'll see how they produce clothing for zara wasin zarya goes every day to my dia this former Levi's employee invested 1500 euros to set up a textile factory today thank you God I have 350 employees and I work with the inditex group Waseem's company. group what seems company is called sip confection it mainly manufactures clothes for Zara hi how are you here as in the entire textile industry in Tunisia 85% of employees are women the workers are between 16 and 35 years old and work We'll be right back. Here, as in the entire textile industry in Tunisia, 85% of employees are women. The workers are between 16 and 35 years old and work 48 hours a week, in accordance with the Tunisian law that Waseem respects religiously, as Inditex is quite strict on that point. Right now, all the employees are working on a very big order of shirts for Zahra. Waseem and his foreman must quickly deliver 10,000 pieces. Give me a shirt. This is a Zahra shirt. It's a woman's shirt. This order is important for Waseem and the future of the the factory there is no way to deliver a poorly finished product so he checks everything and here the girl she does the launching labeling each piece ironing is done here this This room makes 1,200 pieces per day and 150 pieces per hour to achieve such a performance each worker is timed it's called working to the minute for the shirt they have 38 minutes 38 minutes for the finished shirt there who is responsible for the production she does the calculation because beyond 38 minutes the company would lose money the workers are therefore subject to regular checks Jamila does not take her eyes off her watch most factories work like this to calculate the cost per minute of work allows a a maximum of profitability and reduces the prices. Here, workers who perform well will receive a 45-euro bonus at the end of the year. Thanks to these low production costs, Waseem manages to make a profit. But Zara is the true winner. This shirt will be sold 29 euros. three times its production cost here. But Tunisia's reputation as a manufacturer comes from its dyeing factories. And that's a real problem. Masdour, 20 kilometers from Monastir, this is where Waseem outsources the dyeing of his Zara shirt. Maya Dayourari is the director of denim services where a hundred employees work. Mahmoud come here. Here clothes are dyed and jeans faded. Here's the machine where we take Take of the Zara shirts. We find the Zara shirt ready to be dyed in ecru. Those are the dyes. They are direct dyes. For example, you have the pink hue and the beige. For example, for this Zara shirt, the beige. And here's the final result. Cost of the operation, 2 euros. Charged by Demnem services to Waseem. To obtain this color, many toxic products are used. Chemical agents, fixators, acetic acid, all very corrosive to the skin, mucus, membranes, or the lungs. It is dangerous. It can easily attack the stomach. The majority of diseases, cancers, they come from this product. Yet here, those products are handled with bare hands and without any protection. Management does not seem too concerned. Why do you not wear gloves? He says it's not practical. Yes, but it's dangerous for your skin. He says not practical. He wants to work fast, fast. He thinks that the glove is not practical to work. As everywhere, performance is vital for these workers. If they do not work fast enough, they risk to lose their jobs. So it is the priority. They could care less about their health. If those chemicals are used for dyeing are toxic, they are also catastrophic for the environment. Yet, most textile factories are located along the bay of Monastir. Mouni Hassin, president of the Tunisian Forum for Economic and Social Rights, denounces this ecological disaster. Look at this. This is the water that washed genes. It's poured directly here. It arrives from the factories there. There's a gene processing factory in front of us and there's another and there are three there who pour into this canal. Here's the contributions made by those factories that are dumping here directly. And it goes directly into the bay, haphazardly, just like that, you see? Look at the coast there. All those plants. Before they were not there. It was safe. And this bay was an asset. Monastir's Bay was renamed the Triangle of Death. Formerly known for its pure waters and the abundance of fish, the sea has become a dumping ground where untreated waste and chemicals are discharged. The whole world wants to buy cheap clothes, and it has a cost. Unfortunately, the environmental protection is not the government's priority. The authorities are aware of this, but they don't do anything about it because they connect the pollution problem with the employment problem. And they say, what can we do? If we press those factories there, there'll be more unemployment. And we're in a phase where we try to alleviate unemployment and all that. Amancio Ortega employs 150,000 people around the globe. Couldn't he make sure that the factories of his suppliers respect the basic social and environmental rules? Would this question be on Ortega's agenda today? It looks like a normal day in Artesio in Galicia. He heads to his desk at Inditex, like every morning. But in fact, not exactly. The self-made man just turned 80 today. To celebrate this occasion, thousands of employees organized a huge surprise for him. He has no idea what awaits him. Ladies and gentlemen, please welcome Mr. Ortega! It's an intense moment for the little poor kid from Coruña who turned into the emperor of the fashion retailer world. The magnet is unable to hide his tears in front of so much attention. These images are incredible. After decades of hiding from the media, the secret man is finally revealed, appearing in a touching way that we could have not imagined. There's a party going on right here A celebration to last without the heat So bring your good times and your laughter too We're gonna celebrate your party with you Come on and Let's all celebrate and have a good time come on we're gonna have a good time tonight and celebrate are you ready it's alright at 13 At 13, Amanso Ortega promised himself that his mom would never be humiliated again because she was too poor. At 80 years old, he became the richest man in the world. It's up to you What's your pleasure With everyone And find the one To follow Thank you.

timestamps.text: We all know Zara. This brand dresses women, men, and children all over the world. Let's just go to Zara. I know I can find something. They have very nice stuff that you don't find anywhere else. Zara is very special and modern and fashion. Every time I go there, I find new things. Zara, because they have fashion at low cost. In 40 years, it became worldwide with 200 selling points on every continent and new stores that are sprouting everywhere. Zara is one of the rare companies that has a really good communication team in order to not communicate. Zara is the crown jewel of Inditex company, the first textile group in the world. Its headquarters looks like a fortress where only a few exterior people have had the privilege to enter. I had the slight impression of being in a James Bond movie when you enter the villain's lair. Zara is enigmatic, like its founder, Amancio Ortega, who took Bill Gates' title of richest man in the world. Very few people know him. He's 80 years old now. He wanted to democratize fashion. He came from a very poor family. So how did this man revolutionize fashion and build an empire? To tell his story is a challenge because Ortega is unapproachable. But Will unveiled his system, his dazzling success and also his dark side. We will explore the world according to Zara. First, let's go in the austere and remote region where it all began, in the 1960s. Direction northwest of Spain, in the province of Galicia, away from main roads. A Coruña is a port of 250,000 inhabitants. Amancio Ortega grew up there. Today, the strategic center of Zara, in DTEC's headquarters, is still in Ortega, about 10 kilometers from the
port. It is a fortress where one does not enter. In all families in the area, someone has worked
for Inditex. But in A Coruña, we do not talk. And Ortega is a mystery, almost a fantasy.
But we have an appointment with an expert, Javier Blanco. Journalist in A Coruña,
he's the author of the unauthorized biography of Ortega, From Zero to Zara. But we have an appointment with an expert, Javier Blanco, journalist in Acorunia.

He's the author of the unauthorized biography of Ortega, From Zero to Zara. And immediately, he describes an enigmatic man. He's a person who prefers anonymity, a person who does not like to appear in the media. In 1998, a Portuguese newspaper, the Diário de Notícias, had come to question the existence of Amancio. That's why when the group wanted to enter the stock exchange,
they had to publish a photo to reassure future investors and show that there was someone
behind the company. Amancio Ortega is one of the most secret men in the world. Since Mr. Christensen, I have a question for you, Mr. Christensen.

I have a question for you, Mr. Christensen. I have a question for you, Mr. Christensen. Había una persona que estaba detrás de la empresa. Talking about him, without advertising, he amassed a huge fortune, about 80 billion dollars, yet he started from nothing. His story is like a novel. It's the story of a poor boy, Amancio Ortega, who became the richest man in the world. Everything starts in the Coruña in the 50s, when his parents came to settle in the region, where under the regime of General Franco, a dictatorship. Aio son of an employee of the railways is the youngest in a family of four it is in this accommodation for railway workers that he grew up con vadonga she is a legendary fashion journalist in Spain. It is my dear Amancio Ortega. She had the privilege of recording the only interview he has ever given. She drew an authorized biography from it, in which she tells the incredible journey of this young, disadvantaged man who had made the promise at 13 never to be poor again. The day he told me this story, we were eating, and his eyes were filled with tears. I asked him why he was engaged in this business, and he said the truth is that day when my mother picked me up at school, we went in a grocery store where my mother would buy what we were eating for dinner, and the owner said, Josefa, we can't give you credit anymore. He realized how a slap it was to his mother, a humiliation, and it also meant that this, they would not eat. At that moment, he said, this will not happen to my mother ever again. This episode, that could have remained an anecdote, was the first trigger that will propel the young Amancio in the world of fashion. Without any graduation,

he quit school to earn a living. He soon left school and looked for work. As he was not the required age, he had been hired as a handy boy in a shirt store, the gala shop and everything started from there.
In the early 60s, Amanso Ortega left Gala for Maja. a shirt store, the gala shop and everything started from there.
In the early 60s, Amanso Ortega left Gala for Maja, the chic store of A Coruña.
He learned the craft of tailoring.

Above all things, he had a revelation. In the world of fashion, the creators command customers what they should wear. Amanso realized he must do the opposite, take from the customer's taste to give them what they want. For example, there is in the story of a young woman who is in love with a man who is in

But its high price made it accessible only to wealthy clients. He decided to copy it to sell it cheaper. Great idea because at the time it was an essential cloth for women in Galicia. At the time there was no heating in the houses it was very cold and the women used to go down to the streets to shop in their robes. They made a robe that made sense. They abandoned the old concept which actually was just a bag with sleeves, they brought style with a fairly reasonable quality and relatively affordable price. To get started, Ortega, who could not sew, used Rosalia Mera, a saleswoman at Majaha who will soon become his bride. Amancio was an employee of the Maha, a very good employee. Rosalia knew sewing because it happened at a time when everyone in the houses of Spain had sewing machines and made alterations at home. It was a time when everything was left to do, and they dreamt to do something. Amancio then realized he was no longer going to rise at the Maja. To fulfill their dream, Rosalia, Amancio, and his stepsister, Primitiva, work hard. They are young and penniless, but full of determination. They keep their jobs at the Maja, and when they have free time, move into Antonio's dining room, Amancio's brother, to sew gowns. They reproduce the model all women dream of at Coruña, but with a much lower price. Amancio sells them door-to-door. By bicycle and with a very old car, he went from place to place to sell, because it started in Galicia, first in La Coruña. He worked very hard, because in the beginning, he had no money at all. He told me he had borrowed 2,000 pesetas to the bank of Bilbao. 2,000 pesetas is about 30 euros. He asked for a loan because he had nothing. By copying a robe to sell it cheaper to women of A Coruña, answering their desires, Ortega has developed its basic concept. His business is success he sets up a first workshop he calls Goa and starts hiring at Arte show we meet one of Amancio Ortega's first seamstresses Sonia Pinero she's retired but wishes to introduce us to Magdalena another former seamress, but still active. We wait for her at the exit of Inditex factories. We climb directly in the car because Magdalena is afraid to be noticed. At Inditex, we do not talk to the press. As Sonia, Magdalena started working very young at Goa. It brings up the beginnings of Ortega.
Ortega worked alongside his people, his employees.
He always wanted to be there.
If there were boxes to fill, clothes to hang or marks to make, he would do it.

Magdalena wants to show us the modest workshop where Ortega had installed his first company. In Noia Street, in the historic center of Acherunia. Here began the manufacture of Goa confections, the first workshop. This is where I started to work In 1973
Right here?
Yes
Yes, here
You were very young, how old were you?
Thirteen
Thirteen?

But was it legal to work at that age? With the signing of your parents, yes. Is it like that today still? No, it's not. He said he Everybody was working like a mule. and he made the others work like mules, too. He admitted it, but he believed in what he was doing. The business is growing fast, and in 1963, Amancio Ortega opened his first factory, Goa, in which he continues to manufacture sleepwear. Sonia Pinera started working with him at the age of 14. I did not want to study and at home we needed money. I was told, go ahead, they hire people, give it a try. And indeed, the first day I went there, I stayed there.
I did a test and I stayed.
And I spent my life there.
I got married there.
Sonia kept a copy of the first night shirts they made at the time.

They were all more or less like this. It's a night shirt with short sleeves that matched with a bathrobe. To Mr. Ortega, it was clear that the work had to be done well. He sewed buttons, linings, he went to the warehouse. It did not happen often, but if you had collars to iron because an order had to leave, he would begin to iron. But the work does not explain everything. Ortega had flair to surround himself with the best. A key man accompanied him in his rise. Jose Maria Castellano was the son of a driver from A Coruña but it was a very good man very complete he began helping Ortega as an outside counsel he was not in the business until Amancio realized that he was the person he needed Cast, who worked at IBM, convinced Ortega to computerize his business. They started 20 years before most of their competitors. And that was another factor of success. With this strategy, Ortega decided to diversify and produced all kinds of clothes. In 1975, he opened his first boutique in the heart of Akarunia. He baptized it Zara, in reference to Zadar, Croatia's harbor where he spent his honeymoon with Rosalia. This shop still exists. At first, it was said that Zara was not very good quality, but it was cheap.
Sonia remembers very well how they developed the collections.
I remember many magazines from Barcelona.
Well, we bought them in Barcelona, but they were not Spanish.
They were French, Italian.

If you went in the design section, there were piles of magazines everywhere. And he copied the models. Yes, indeed. The store in A Coruña was the first of a very long list. Ortega extended his empire gradually. Each store opened following the truck's route, which means the truck first stopped in Zamora, then Salamanca, next Casas, then Badajoz, and Sevilla. It followed the truck's route. The first store Amancio opened abroad was in Portugal, 150 kilometers from Galiz. He did not open in New York, but in Portugal. He colonized little by little. 40 years later, Zara is an empire with more than 2,000 stores in the world, but didn't change its methods. Like before, the brand copies bigger brands' shape, colors, and styles, because this is how they succeeded.
Amancio started by copying.
And there's quite a story about that.
He had a friend who bought a shirt in Italy and wanted to wear it to a wedding.
She bumped into Amancio wearing that shirt and begged, please don't copy it.
But he didn't say he was copying.
There's a part in my book where I tell him,
Amancio, you copy everything you see.
And he answers, no, I'm inspired by it.
He told me that one day.

He was in his car and he saw a young man on a bike wearing a jacket with all those medals. He looked at it and called the design director right away. He described what he saw in detail and they made a lot of money with this model. Some fashion websites claim that Zara is copying luxurious brands. And they follow their claims with pictures. Like this pair of shoes sold 720 euros at Prada. Those are the ones sold at Zara. Or look-a-like. They're sold 80 euros. Nine times cheaper.
And those websites have a whole lot of examples.
Here's an Isabelle Marant top. Look at the one from Zara. Or those shoes. Try to guess. Which are Louboutin? Which are Zara?
Facing those accusations of plagiarism, the brand decided not to comment.

The real question is to know where does the inspiration stop and copy begin. Many small independent designers think Zara and other textiles giants took from their creations. But it is too difficult to fight against those giants. In the heart of Paris, we visit the studio of ready-to-wear brand G. Carrow. I'll leave the skirt here. Yes, I'll take a look at it later. Siblings, Philippe and Marguerite Barterotte, launched their brand G. Carrow five years ago. An original style quickly noticed for its printed and colorful shirts.

They caught the attention of the public, but also of other brands, including Zara. Since, as soon as Marguerite draws new creations, imitations appear on the market, it keeps her up at night. it's really stealing it's a bit like i was an old lady in the street with my bag and someone grabs it and left makes me sad it makes me want to scream here's an example of what drives marguerite and her brother crazy the first time i think we've been copied, it was our birds shirt.
The birds is a very simple shirt, adding these blue birds like that on a shirt.
It's Marguerite, the creator of G. Kero, who invented it.

Now this bird pattern on a shirt, you can find it on a Zara model with the same layout and the same colors coincidence perhaps anyway a few collections later when Marguerite Barthelot draws ballerinas so did Zara and the height for this model is that the Spanish brand beat them to the punch. You can find this shirt in stores. It's already on sale, while for us, it's not out yet. It's already out at Zara. For the young brand, it is difficult to deal with the force of a world leader that benefits alone from the novelty effect. We present our collections almost a year in advance, a year before they're sold. And the retail chains such as Zara, they send people to sniff out what's going on in the shows, so a year in advance. And with their production capacity, they can produce something in only two months. So if they decide to copy something, they'll get a copy out before the original comes out.
Philippe is concerned about the future of his company, especially because others have followed Zara's plan.
This is the Monoprix Zebra and this is ours. And this could be Geekero, but it's ProMod.
Here's Marguerite and Philippe's version.
You must look at the details to see the difference.
The graphic composition's been copied.
They dressed the characters up because they couldn't show the Kama Sutra.
They changed the color.
See this little line under here?
It's exactly below.
You have the triangles, and you have the same spacing.

If you're not known, you're a little designer, and you're copied two, three, four, five times. People never talk about you. You see all the copies promoted in the press. It doesn't hurt just a little. It's not a loss for us. It's a death sentence. G.K.R. decided to attack Zarek for counterfeiting and unfair competition, but they committed themselves to a long and costly process.
In front of such findings, we asked Zarek's opinion. We sent an email to Inditex.
We noticed that several brands accused you of copying some of their models.

We found surprising similarities between models of the French brand G. Kero and Zarek's. We would like to have your opinion on it. To date, Zara did not respond to our email. One might think that in the fashion industry, to copy a competitor is taking the risk of being sued and a huge fine. But to prove to a judge that there has been copy is much more complex than we think. For example, for this coat, Sandra Brand has filed a complaint against Zara. They were pointing many similarities. The top of the leather jacket, biker style,
the snaps at the end, and the wool. The French justice condemned Zara to pay 200,000 euros

to Sandro. But don't think that it's always like that because the brands that have lost against Zara are numerous some very prestigious like famous French shoe designer Christian Louboutin we recognize his creations across the world for their famous red soul found on all models he even copyrighted it so one might think that louboutin is the only brand allowed to make this its personal touch but look at this pair of zara shoes inspired by this model from louboutin same shape and red soul the parisian shoemaker decided to sue but after years of proceedings, he was ordered to pay €2,500 damages to Zara. In short, Louboutin doesn't have the red-soled monopoly. In Paris, we went to ask the opinion of a fashion expert.
Writer, journalist, popular figure on Instagram,
Sophie Fontanel spent 20 years at Elle magazine.
We asked her what she thinks of Zara and plagiarism.
We must know one thing.

The creators, when they invent something, inspire themselves from something else. So this is a very important point. Zara calls it sourcing. Those people who go to the source of things. There are people who score the flea markets, who go to thrift stores, who buy clothes. And as there are people who know fashion really well, they know that the 70s are coming back and that the 80s are coming back. So when we attack Zara and tell them, you imitated me, Zara can say, wait, it was anticipated. Yes, I put rabbits on a shirt. But look, this shirt from the 70s also has rabbits. For her, imitating and repeating is the base of the fashion system. Before copying and imitation, there's just doing again, which is the history of clothing. There is a phrase from Giacometti that says, I only make by destroying. Well by remaking copying is difficult to prove so Zara continues to draw inspiration from the creations of others Zara's customers know and that is among other things what they come picking in store the clothes resemble those of the big brands but cheaper Zara captures all the trends to make them come to the store. It's copy and paste sometimes. Copies are immediate. When something new comes out, they already have it. Zara takes from leading brands to develop its products. It's obvious. They make copies, yes, but good good copies sometimes they copy a little for
example the Koopal shirt yes Zara is inspired by maybe you have a crush on
this little Isabelle Moran top those Chloe boots or this Dior dress well you

can find almost the same models at Zara, but much cheaper. We tried to contact the press services of these brands, but they refused to answer. For them, it is simply bad publicity. But Zara is not only drawing from the biggest names in luxury, it also took over their codes. For example, it does everything to make us think we're in a top-of-the-range store and it starts with the appearance of its showcases in the fashion world the showcases give the image of the brand Zara's understood that and integrated it from the start in its strategy and it is in the bunker of a coruna that showcases are designed, in great secret. Mark Tungate is a British writer. Ten years ago, he published a book on the world of fashion. On this occasion, he had the rare privilege of entering the premises of Inditex, a very difficult place to enter. I had the slight impression of being in a James Bond movie when you arrive at the villain's lair.
There was a lift going up, you arrive in a lobby, everything was white, white, white, very minimalist.
They have a whole street of showcases, which are called pilot stores.
It's like a ghost town.
You see a street of Zara stores with different showcases being set up.

And they create with their team the image that will be the image of the season and that will be transmitted to all the shops around the world. Those ghost stores are unique in the fashion world. In the real life, the brand cares about getting the best locations. It reportedly paid 300 million euros for the store located on Fifth Avenue in New York. Zara invests heavily in its stores. Those displays you immediately notice are located in the nicest neighborhoods. It is Zara's best publicity. To To understand the Zara system, we take an appointment in Paris with a man working for a competing company.
For months, he studied Zara's operations.
For him, the brand's inner workings are frightening.
He agreed to meet in a discreet place, anonymously.
I tried to crack the model
to understand how this model works and what i could learn for my own business
when looking at the financial model and more particularly the communication and advertising
budget we realize that there is a line with zero so there are no expenses in advertising displays
or television such positioning is unique in the fashion world because the textile market It is highly recommended that you try it.

It in the fashion world, because the textile market is highly competitive and is normally based on advertising. For example, H&M, Zara's closest competitor, calls in singers of football players to create a buzz. Like other retailers, the Swedish brand spends around 3.5% of its turnover in advertising campaigns. Not Zara. Zara is not a brand that is worn on red carpets, so they went for a low profile. They build themselves on other people's desires. It really is a coveted brand. Why are brands advertising? It is in case they are not desired, you see? It is to prevent this potential lack of desire or to stir it. They didn't do that. Zara is in all aspects a different brand. To limit it for its imitation or marketing would be a mistake. The truth is that Mansoor Tiga is a visionary who revolutionized fashion. With Zara, he invented the fast fashion. They produce and deliver in record time.
